claim_pattern: Verse A quotes / alludes to / fulfills verse B
needed_math: Parse the actual WEB Bible text. A quotation is corroborated when a contiguous substring (≥ N tokens, allowing for translation drift) from verse B appears in verse A, or the engine can compute textual cosine similarity above a stated threshold using only the public-domain WEB and LXX corpora the engine already holds at Layer 0.
needed_substrate: Layer 0 WEB Bible (have), LXX (have via lw/00_source), token-similarity computation (need)
why_not_now: The TSK seed was Torrey's editorial judgment — authority, not math. Removed.
close_note: Added scripture.quotation: resolves both verses to WEB text, computes the longest contiguous shared phrase + a content-word overlap coefficient (stopwords stripped). Confirms quotation/allusion (textual math); DECLINES 'fulfills' (interpretation, not text). Live-verified on the real WEB: Acts 7:49 / Isaiah 66:1 CONFIRMED (7-token phrase, 0.83); Matthew 4:4 / Deuteronomy 8:3 CONFIRMED; John 3:16 / Genesis 1:1 MISMATCH. Wired into scripture.run() via QUOTATION_VERIFY; classifier extraction of the quotation spec is the downstream follow-up.

claim_pattern: Humans landed on / could have reached the Moon
example_claim: Apollo 11 reached the Moon on July 20, 1969
needed_math: (1) Tsiolkovsky rocket equation: Δv = v_e · ln(m_0 / m_f). Given Saturn V's documented stage masses and Isp values as INPUTS the operator supplies (not as engine-confirmed truths), compute total Δv and confirm it exceeds Earth-to-lunar-injection Δv. (2) Ephemeris (have): Earth-Moon distance on the claimed date. (3) Lunar laser ranging: timing measurements taken since 1969 continue to be consistent with retroreflectors at the Apollo sites; if those timing values are supplied, the engine can check the round-trip time against c × 2 × distance.
needed_substrate: None new — physics already shipped. Worker needs to compose the calls.
why_not_now: Engine doesn't say 'happened' from an encyclopedia lookup. Operator must supply rocket specs and laser-ranging data as evidence; engine confirms the math closes.

claim_pattern: Substance X boils / melts at temperature Y at pressure P
needed_math: Clausius-Clapeyron equation: dP/dT = L / (T · ΔV). Given a substance's latent heat of vaporization L, gas constant R, and a reference (T_ref, P_ref), compute the boiling temperature at any pressure. L itself is a measured quantity, but it can be supplied as input by the operator and the engine checks the equation. Confirms the relationship, not the absolute value.
needed_substrate: Latent heat values as inputs (substance-specific). Constant R already in CODATA.
why_not_now: Removed the table-lookup verifier (water 100°C, iron 1538°C). On the engine's build queue until Clausius-Clapeyron math is wired.
close_note: Added thermodynamics.clausius_clapeyron: ln(P2/P1)=(L/R)(1/T1-1/T2); given operator-supplied L + reference point, predicts boiling T at a pressure and confirms the relationship. Bare phase-point lookups remain OUT_OF_SCOPE by design. Unit-verified (water at 70 kPa -> ~363 K).

claim_pattern: Element X has atomic mass Y u
needed_math: Atomic mass is a weighted average of isotopic masses by natural abundance. Math: m_avg = Σ (abundance_i × mass_i). The individual isotope masses come from mass-spectrometry measurement; the abundance values come from terrestrial-sample analysis. Engine confirms the weighted-average computation given the (isotope_mass, abundance) pairs as INPUTS.
needed_substrate: Engine doesn't store isotope masses or abundances. Operator would supply them per-claim, or a public-domain isotopic-composition table would need to be sourced from authoritative measurement (NIST atomic weights).
why_not_now: Atomic mass verification was lookup against IUPAC published values, not derivation. Removed atomic_mass check from periodic_table; kept atomic_number which IS definitional.
close_note: Added periodic_table.atomic_mass_weighted_average: confirms Sum(abundance*mass) from operator-supplied isotopic inputs; never looks up mass. Unit-verified.

claim_pattern: X is a valid IANA timezone identifier
example_claim: America/New_York is a valid IANA timezone
needed_math: None. Whether the IANA tz committee accepted a name is an authority claim. The engine doesn't confirm such facts. The utc_offset computation (given a tz name) IS math and survives — that one is kept.
why_not_now: This is intrinsically an authority question. Build target may be 'never' — the engine can decline to verify this kind of claim entirely.

claim_pattern: 100g of food X has Y kcal / Z g protein
example_claim: 100g of cooked brown rice has 112 kcal
needed_math: Calorimetry: ΔU = m · c · ΔT. Given measured combustion enthalpy and ash composition for a specific lab sample, compute the kcal. The kcal is the integral of (4 kcal/g · protein) + (4 kcal/g · carb) + (9 kcal/g · fat) — the Atwater factors — which engine could apply IF given the macronutrient grams. But the grams themselves come from chemical assay of a specific sample. Engine could confirm the Atwater computation, not the substrate values.
needed_substrate: Operator supplies macronutrient grams; engine applies Atwater factors.
why_not_now: USDA's database is measurement on one set of samples. Different samples vary. Removed lookup verifier. On the build queue as 'Atwater-factor math given operator-supplied grams.'
close_note: verify_macronutrient_calories already wired in nutrition.run() (Atwater 4-4-9-7); unit-verified.

claim_pattern: US inflation / unemployment / fed funds rate / GDP in year Y was X%
needed_math: Inflation is defined as the rate of change of a price index. Math: inflation_t = (CPI_t - CPI_{t-1}) / CPI_{t-1}. Given two CPI index values as INPUTS, engine confirms the computed rate. The CPI level itself is a measured aggregation by BLS — authority. Engine can verify the rate calculation, not the underlying level.
needed_substrate: Operator supplies the two index values; engine computes the rate.
why_not_now: FRED seed was authority lookup ('BLS said inflation was X%'). Removed. Build target: rate-of-change math given operator-supplied index values.
close_note: Added economics.inflation_rate = (CPI_t - CPI_prev)/CPI_prev; accepts fraction or percent; unit-verified.

claim_pattern: The capital of country X is Y
example_claim: The capital of France is Paris
needed_math: None. Capitals are political/legal designations established by constitutions and treaties. Engine could verify that the country's constitution (a public-domain document) states the capital, but that reduces to text retrieval from a curated corpus — still an authority chain.
why_not_now: This is intrinsically a political-authority question. Build target may be 'never' — the engine can decline to verify such facts.

claim_pattern: Person X was born / died in year Y
needed_math: None for general historical figures. For Biblical figures, engine can use Layer 0 (the Bible) where dates / generations are textually given — but those are still textual claims, not first-principles math.
why_not_now: Wikidata-style biographical lookup is authority, not math. Build target may be limited to Biblical chronology where Layer 0 supplies textual evidence directly.

claim_pattern: Event X happened in year Y
example_claim: The Battle of Hastings was in 1066
needed_math: None for general events. For astronomically-anchored events (eclipses, equinoxes, planetary alignments), the engine's ephemeris can compute the date; if a historical record claims an event happened on the day of an eclipse, the engine can verify the eclipse occurred that day.
why_not_now: Most historical-event-year claims have no math chain back to first principles. A narrow class — events tied to astronomical observations — could be verified.

claim_pattern: X is the speed of light Y m/s
example_claim: The capital of France is Paris.
needed_math: Already covered by physical_constants verifier. Test that engine catches a user typo / lie about the value.
close_note: Verifier-level catch PROVEN: physical_constants.value flags speed_of_light=3.0e8 as MISMATCH (diff 207542 >> tol 29979); truth 299792458 CONFIRMED; alias 'c' works. Regression cases added (truth + lie). NOTE: end-to-end catch depends on bq-classifier-no-autocorrect (still open) — the LLM classifier can auto-correct a user's wrong value before the verifier sees it; this test guards that fix.

claim_pattern: User submits a deliberately wrong claim (lie / typo / mistake)
needed_math: Post-process Claude's spec output: for each numeric value in the spec, verify the number appears in the user's original claim text (allowing for scientific-notation / unit-conversion equivalents). When a spec value is NOT present in the source text, that means Claude silently substituted the correct value before the verifier could check it. Mark such results as suspicious — convert CONFIRMED to a flagged state, or re-run extraction with stricter constraints (e.g. structured output schema with the source-text quotation as a required field). The regression suite at scripts/regression/run_regression.py catches this; 4 of 30 claims currently fail because Haiku helpfully corrects user errors before the verifier sees them.
needed_substrate: None — pure post-processing on the existing spec output
why_not_now: Identified by regression suite 2026-05-11. Even with explicit examples in the classifier prompt showing WRONG vs RIGHT extraction, Claude Haiku continues to auto-correct obvious user errors. A reliable fix needs structural verification of the spec values against the source text, not prompting alone.
close_note: Structural grounding guard: _spec_grounded_in_source checks every numeric spec value is traceable (within power-of-ten) to the user's ORIGINAL text. The two-stage classify path was already guarded; extended the guard to the single-call _poly_oracle_combined fallback. Ungrounded -> block/quarantine, never silently confirm a substituted value. Unit-verified: 'iron atomic number 99' flags spec value 26 and blocks on BOTH paths.
